summary: DHH calls for Google to immediately pull its Web Accelerator product, which was causing data loss and destructive side effects for web applications. He compares the situation to automotive recalls for faulty vehicles. The post argues that the real test of Google's 'don't be evil' motto isn't that they made a mistake, but how quickly they respond to fix it. He uses a Fight Club reference to satirize the corporate cost-benefit calculation of whether to issue a recall.
key_points: Google's Web Accelerator was causing data loss and destructive behavior for web applications
The measure of a company's character is not avoiding mistakes but responding swiftly when they happen
Google's 'do no evil' motto faces its real test in how they handle their own screw-ups
Corporate cost-benefit analysis on whether to recall a harmful product is cynical and unacceptable
money_quotes: Google's Web Accelerator is causing death and destruction on an admitted much lower level of criticality, but it too is in need of an immediate recall.
Google screwed up on this one. Really bad.
The trial of "do no evil" is how quickly and effectively Google can react to their screw-up and set things right.
Google needs to acknowledge the size of X immediately and act on it yesterday.
key_insight: A tech company's true character is revealed not by whether it ships faulty products, but by how quickly it acts to fix the damage when it does.
